data.title: Blue Owl Capital: Why I Am Buying The Drop
data.description:
Blue Owl Capital (NYSE:OBDC) completed its merger with Blue Owl Capital Corporation III at the start of the year and shares have seen some negative momentum since the BDC submitted its fourth-quarter earnings sheet. In the fourth fiscal quarter, Blue Owl Capital also saw improving balance sheet quality as well as decent dividend coverage based off of net investment income. Shares are still one of the best deals in the BDC sector, in my opinion, and I believe Blue Owl Capital has potential for a revaluation in FY 2025, especially since the BDC remains focused on variable rate loans and the Federal Reserve indicated that it will keep interest rates higher for longer.

Previous rating

I rated shares Blue Owl Capital a strong buy in my last coverage on the BDC in December -- A Magnificent 10% Yield -- because I liked the value proposition from a balance sheet quality and dividend coverage perspective, but also saw upside potential related to the completion of the merger with Blue Owl Capital Corporation III. In the fourth-quarter, Blue Owl Capital continued to support its dividend with net investment income and quality in the portfolio improved. I consider Blue Owl Capital's 10% yield to be of high-quality and believe the investment firm will grow its dividend in the future.

Blue Owl Capital is a leading BDC with a strong investment proposition and improving loan quality

Blue Owl Capital is leading investment company in the middle market segment in the U.S. The BDC recently merged its operations with those of Blue Owl Capital Corporation III and has become one of the largest BDCs in the market with a portfolio valued at $13.2B and a market cap of $7.8B.

Like most BDCs, Blue Owl Capital runs a first lien-strategy with 76% of all investments falling into the first lien category. Equity represented 15% of investments (based off of fair value), followed by second liens (5%) and unsecured debt and joint ventures with a 2% investment share each.

In terms of balance sheet quality, Blue Owl Capital had a decent quarter as well: the non-accrual percentage, which measures the amount of non-performing loans as a percentage of total outstanding loans, fell 0.3 PP quarter-over-quarter to 0.4%. For the investment firm, it was the third consecutive Q/Q drop in the non-accrual percentage since non-accruals (at fair value) peaked in Q1’24.

$M

Q4'23

Q1'24

Q2'24

Q3'24

Q4'24

Total Investments

$12,713

$12,414

$13,342

$13,448

$13,195

Non-Accrual (at cost)

1.3%

2.5%

3.1%

2.1%

1.9%

Non-Accrual (at fair value)

1.1%

1.8%

1.4%

0.7%

0.4%

(Source: Author)

Blue Owl Capital generated $184.2M in net investment income ($0.47 per-share) in the December quarter, showing a decline of 7% year-over-year, mainly due to lower income related to variable rate loans. Almost all of Blue Owl Capital's loan are paying the BDC a variable rate, so a decline in the federal fund rate in FY 2025 could accelerate this trend.

Blue Owl Capital's dividend coverage ratio in the fourth-quarter was 1.27X compared to 1.28X in the trailing-twelve months period and 1.46X (the dividend coverage ratios exclude the payment of supplemental dividends). Based off of the current dividend coverage ratios, I see upside potential for Blue Owl Capital's dividend in the future.

Blue Owl Capital trades at an attractive P/NAV ratio...

In my last work on Blue Owl Capital I said this:

If Blue Owl Capital revalued to a range of 1.10-1.15X price-to-NAV ratio, which I consider to be more realistic given the quality of the portfolio and Q3'24 performance sheet, then the investment company could have a fair value in the range of $16.80-17.50 per-share.

Shares of Blue Owl Capital currently trade at a price-to-NAV ratio of 1.0X -- based off of a net asset value of $15.26 per-share -- which makes the BDC one of the more affordable BDCs for investors, especially with the loan quality profile looking as solid as it does. If we apply this multiplier range to Blue Owl Capital's last net asset value we can calculate a fair value between $16.79 and $17.55 per-share.

Blackstone Secured Lending (BXSL) is trading at a 20% premium to net asset value compared to a 4% discount to net asset value for FS KKR Capital (FSK). FS KKR Capital has had its own issues with its portfolio quality, but the non-accrual percentage has slowly improved in the last several quarters which means I continue to see re-pricing upside for this BDC.

Ares Capital (ARCC) is priced at a 16% premium to net asset value and I recently down-graded the BDC due to valuation concerns. My current rating on Ares Capital is hold and I recommend waiting for a drop below $22.87 per-share before buying into the BDC.

Risks with Blue Owl Capital

Blue Owl Capital is a variable rate BDC with significant exposure to interest rates. A decline in the federal fund rate, which is likely to occur in 2025, will add pressure to the BDC’s net investment income, the majority of which (94%) is derived from interest income from its variable rate loans. What would change my mind about Blue Owl Capital is if the BDC saw an up-trend in its non-accrual percentage or saw a deterioration in its dividend coverage ratio.

Final thoughts

Blue Owl Capital is a well-run BDC and the investment firm just completed its merger with Blue Owl Capital Corporation III in January. The fourth-quarter was a solid quarter for OBDC in terms of investment performance as the firm: the BDC’s balance sheet quality improved with the non-accrual percentage dropping to just 0.4%. Shares are now trading at just about net asset value which is great considering that the BDC has very strong dividend coverage. I see room for both a high NAV multiplier and a higher dividend. In my opinion, Blue Owl Capital represents the most attractive high-yield investment opportunity in the industry group here, and I see a favorable risk profile for the BDC as well.

data.title: TeraWulf Inc. 2024 Q4 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by TeraWulf Inc. in conjunction with their 2024 Q4 earnings call.

data.title: AI's Impact On Dell: Performance, Prediction, And Pitfalls (Q4 Earnings Review)
data.description:
Introduction

Dell (NYSE:DELL) has usually delivered moderately satisfying results. But in 2023 the stock suddenly awakened and delivered outstanding results through mid-2024, reaching a 5-yr return above 450%.

Since then, the stock has been quite volatile and is finding some support around $100, though it still has to be seen whether this level will hold. What made Dell such a successful stock last year? The answer is easy: AI. But it is also a very complex answer as well. In fact, AI can mean almost anything when it comes to AI stocks.

In Dell's case, strong demand for AI-optimized servers quickly doubled and created an impressive backlog for many years. Part of the narrative around Dell took this shape: we are in the early innings of a massive investment cycle in AI infrastructure. Since Dell is a leader in servers and storage services, it soon became an investors' favorite. Thanks to its exposure to a strong cycle, Dell started generating billions in FCF and enhanced its dividend and buyback policy. At the same time, it reduced its leverage ratio from a 3.2x to a much better 1.5x in just four years. Let's say it right away: this new report told us the leverage ratio is now 1.2x. Now, despite a surge in AI server demand, Dell had to repeat several times that this business is actually margin rate dilutive, as I explained in my past article. As a result, the sales mix is currently unfavorable to Dell's earnings. After all, competition and inflationary pressure are denting part of Dell's profits. In addition, a few months ago Michael Dell sold $1.2B worth of Dell's stock, and more than one investor raised his eyebrows doubting whether the company's founder and CEO thinks Dell's value might have peaked.

Dell recently released its Q4 earnings report and initiated guidance for this next fiscal year (for Dell, it is fiscal year 2026). Let's dive into the report and see what's happening and what we should expect going forward.

Dell's Q4 Earnings Review

Well, let's start with how the market reacted. After earnings, the stock first rose 3% in extended trading, but then moved down by around 5%: the report was more or less in line with analysts' expectations, but this market is unforgiving and requires strong top and bottom line beats to support stretched valuations. But, let's quickly recall some of the main financials to make it easier for us all to understand the report and gauge the FY26 outlook.

Dell reported $95.6B in FY revenue, up 8% YoY; the operating income of $6.2B was up 15% YoY (6.4% margin), while Dell's FY EPS of $6.38 was up 39% YoY. Operating cash was $4.5B and this led to a dividend boost of 18% supported by a $10B increase in the share repurchase program. As a result, the fwd yield is now 2% and the buyback program is worth north of 13% of the current market cap. It is a solid way to support the stock and assure investors that they will have their nice share of the pie.

However, the Q1 guidance came up short of expectations. Dell forecasts to earn $1.65 per share, while analysts were expecting $1.78.

The trend of a negative sales mix was confirmed: infrastructure solutions keeps growing, while client solutions is declining.

After all, Infrastructure Solutions saw its FY revenue increase 29% YoY to $43.6B, with operating income up 30% to $5.6B. Server revenue took the lion's share of this massive growth and jumped 54% YoY. At the same time, Client Solutions reported a 1% YoY decline to $48.4B, with operating income of $3B down 20% YoY. Consumer revenue performed the worst with a 17% decline, while commercial revenue held up and grew 3% YoY.

Why is the consumer doing poorly? On the earnings call, Dell's management explained it plainly:

Profitability in commercial was weaker than expected as demand continued to push into the next fiscal year. In consumer, the demand environment remains soft and profitability remains challenged. We are ready and well positioned for a PC refresh with our simplified rebrand, leading go-to-market engine and focus on commercial PCs, the most profitable segments of the market.

The new guidance expects Dell to top $100B in sales this year with a range between $101B and $105B and EPS is expected to reach $7.85, which would be a 23% YoY increase. But during the earnings call, we saw a mixed picture regarding the profit opportunity for Dell. In fact, while it was explained and stressed that the AI hardware and services represent an incredible opportunity whose TAM is compounding at 33% annually and will reach the impressive size of almost $300B, we also heard Dell guiding for further gross margin compression by around 100 bps due to a higher sales mix of AI-optimized servers and competition in the consumer segment. But we also should consider that shipments were a bit higher than incoming orders. After all, we are also hearing news of more cautious AI spending across big tech.

Now, the real operational issue that Dell faces is scaling enterprise AI developments, focusing on the return on its investments. However, we need to consider a caveat that I have rarely seen addressed so far.

As the world moves and spends to build AI infrastructure, it is not clear the lifetime value of these investments. Since I often cover Class 1 railroads, allow me an example. When a railroad invests in new tracks, it knows it will have 50+ years to amortize the investment and earn decent returns. On average, railroads have a ROIC between 12% to 15%. For most of the time, it is a return above the cost of capital and makes railroads value-creating businesses. With AI infrastructure, chances are the investments we are seeing today will become obsolete much sooner than 50+ years. A tech cycle seems to be replaced by a new one in five years or so. This poses an issue. Are those companies investing in AI infrastructure going to earn a decent return on their capex in just five years? They might, but it is much more difficult to predict. This creates an issue for Dell, going forward. If AI servers are expensive, it will be harder to keep investing in them if the AI hype fades a bit away and gives way to more prudent spending. Therefore, Dell has a pricing (or at least a cost) issue that it will have to solve if it wants to make its servers more affordable and attractive for customers around the world.

Of course, Dell also has a moat protecting it from new competitors. On the earnings call, we heard once again that "it takes significant engineering capability" to build unique and differentiated solutions for every customer. For example, Dell was the first company that brought to market a GB200 rack in collaboration with NVIDIA.

Valuation

Now, if Dell is following a trend where sales are increasing and margins are pressured, the impact on its multiples is unfavorable. More in detail, we might see a better price/sales ratio, but this is not the most important metric I would use to value Dell. Before AI, Dell used to trade at a low PE of around 10 and a P/FCF of 8. After coming close to 19, the earnings multiple compressed as the stock sold down and entered bear territory since my last article was written in November 2024. But the fwd PE is still at 15.6, which is above the average. This makes sense because the narrative around Dell has changed in recent years and a new premium might be priced in. Its P/FCF is back to an 8. I must honestly admit I don't really understand why Dell's valuation grade is a D- when all its sector-relative grades are As and Bs. To me, the stock is being more fairly priced right now than a few months ago.

However, there is a caveat. In only 6 months, Dell's growth grade has moved from an A to a C, reflecting softer-than-anticipated forecasts. If Dell's growth slows down further and goes back to the low single digits, then we will probably see its earnings multiple compress a bit.

Right now, Dell seems more attractively priced than six months ago and seems to be offering a nice entry point as it tests $100, especially if the price holds, as it seems, it is doing after earnings. However, I would step in gradually because the outlook is not as clear as it seemed to be a few months ago.

data.title: Pebblebrook Hotel Trust Is Risky, But We Are Buying The 8.5% Preferred Stocks
data.description:
“Your life is the result of the choices that you have made; if you don't like your life, then make better choices.” This is a quote I remind myself regularly when deciding on what to invest in. The main issue is when we lack the proper information. That's where we come in. We look at a sector, we analyze it, and then, based on each mentioned company's reports, we make comparisons. Today, we are turning our attention to Pebblebrook Hotel Trust(NYSE:PEB). The company has four exchange-traded preferred stocks outstanding:

  • The 6.38% (PEB.PR.E)
  • The 6.30% (PEB.PR.F)
  • The 5.70% (PEB.PR.G)
  • The 6.38% (PEB.PR.H).

Pebblebrook Hotel Trust invests in upscale hotel and resort properties in major US cities. Some readers might be familiar with their properties:

In the screenshot below, you can see some of the hotel's properties.

You can see the rest of them on their website if you are curious. And as much as I would love to talk about future vacation plans, today's article isn't about that. We are here for their financial metrics.

Financial information:

The company's hotels are valued at $5.4 billion on the balance sheet. Total assets are around $5.7 billion, and PEB's total debt is $2.2 billion. According to their last quarterly report, the preferred shares represent approximately $690,000,000. The trust's common stock trades at 0.51 times book value. If we use the market-adjusted value of the assets, we can easily claim that the company's assets coverage ratio is close to 196%, which is lower than the balance sheet ACR of 259%.

Profitability:

PEB earns around 13% gross from renting its rooms. Approximately 26% of this revenue goes to room-related expenses. If we remove depreciation and amortization from the equation, we can see that operating expenses eat up around 78% of the revenue. We can also calculate an asset yield using the formula: Total revenue — operating expenses + depreciation and amortization/total assets. We ended up getting a 4.87% asset yield. Considering the P/B value of its assets, we can calculate a market-adjusted asset yield of 6.44%. Interest expenses are expected to be around $0.19bln, approximately 8% of the total operating expenses. The interest expense divided by total debt gives us a 5% cost of the debt.

We see an FFO (funds from operations) yield per share of 1.58 / 11.79 (current share price), or 13.4%. This is quite a large expected return, and while one can be tempted by it, we prefer to play it safe with such risky entities.

Preferred stocks:

The company's four exchange-traded stocks have current yields of around 8.50%. The dividends of all preferred stocks are around $34 million yearly. This is close to 15% of the expected FFO, a relatively small amount that provides a large FFO cushion even if management is wrong in its expectations. Even if we add the preferred stocks to the company's overall leverage, we still receive a market-adjusted asset coverage ratio of approximately 170%.

Hotel REIT sector return

Bond Yields.

You can see in the chart above what the Hotel REIT baby bonds yield curve looks like.

Historical pricing of PEB preferreds:

The outstanding PEB preferred stocks were issued at an average spread to the 20-year treasury of around 4%. At the time of writing, the spread is close to its IPO spread. This is contrary to the overall dynamic of narrower spreads, which signals some credit concerns in the pricing of the preferred stocks. We need to examine this. The best way to do it is to use Moody's credit-scoring process.

Credit score evaluation:

Moody's approach to assessing financial strength within the REIT sector is based on the following metrics:

I. Scale and Business Profile

    1. PEB has $7.25 billion of gross assets(including depreciation), which gives it a score of 8.53, or a Baa equivalent.

  1. We decided to give one of the worst possible scores for asset quality and operating environment because Moody's generally believes that the Hotel REIT sector bears the most risk. Here, PEB scores 20 and 20, respectively, which is a Ca equivalent.

II. Liquidity and Access to Capital

  1. For Liquidity and access to capital, the trust gets a score of 10 or a Baa.
  2. PEB has approximately $300 million of encumbered assets(less than 5%), which gives it a score of 4.70 or an A equivalent.

III. Leverage and Coverage

  1. This is the metric with the highest weight of the four. When we calculate the Total debt (including Pref stocks) of $2.22 billion and then proceed to divide it by the company's gross assets of $7.25 billion, we get a score of 8.99 or a Baa equivalent.
  2. Net Debt/EBITDA, in this case, gives us a score of 18.1 or a Caa equivalent
  3. Secured debt represents 2.7% of the assets, which gives us a score of 4.15, which is an Aa equivalent.
  4. Fixed-charge coverage — We ended up getting a score of 14.58, or a B rating. The company's overall credit score equivalent is 12.43, a Ba3 rating equivalent. If we are to rate the preferred stock, it will get a score close to B1. Now, add to this the low P/B multiple of the common stock, and you have to understand that this is not a safe haven. It is difficult to find “Alpha” in the less risky assets, so we are forced to enter junk territory with PEB preferreds and their comparable preferred stocks:

We can see that PEB's preferred stocks offer around a 1.5% higher current yield than companies like LAND, EPR, BFS, SLG, UMH, DBRG, and GOOD. We run the same credit-scoring process for all of these companies, and you can see how their credit scores compare with their yield metrics:

PEB has a relatively low credit score compared to the group, while its CY is the highest. The higher the red column is than the blue column, the higher the “Alpha.” Even though PEB's credit score is well into the high-yield, PEB seems to have the best of the worst preferreds in the comparison. We have our statistical edge as well. After trading these preferreds for many years, I have always viewed them as overvalued:

RLJ-A is the first stock that comes to mind. The market has always viewed PEB and RLJ as if their credibility is similar. The deviation is logical, but we are almost sure that the market will not be rational for long, and we will find PEB preferred stocks being overvalued again (hopefully).

Conclusion

We are taking some risks by buying PEB preferred stock. There are several negative factors to consider. Our biggest issue is the market-adjusted asset coverage ratio. On a relative basis, PEB preferred stocks are priced way worse than peers, and there is some Alpha to be generated. We are waiting for the next report, and hopefully, high risk means high return in this case.

data.title: AT&T: The Next Big Catalyst
data.description:
We have covered AT&T (NYSE:T) more than any other stock in our public coverage universe. We have moved from controversial sell calls, to buys when shares were mid teens, to suggesting running a house position with trading gains to collect all future dividends (and a bullish hold rating to go with it). At BAD BEAT Investing we continue to like selling covered calls and out of the money puts for income generation on top of the bountiful dividend. The stock has been a wonderful performer, and in our last coverage we took a hard look at the earnings, but we are updating on recent news and items regarding AI. Take a look at the chart.

This is a very bullish chart folks, and we continue to like holding a position for the great income. We also believe a dividend hike could be in the cards as cash flow remains strong. We will touch on some performance of the legacy business, but before doing so, we question what the next catalyst could be? Perhaps in bandwagon fashion, we would argue the further incorporation of AI will be the next catalyst. While some AI is already being employed by telecoms for customer assistance and product/service recommendations, having direct incorporation of AI tech through the services offered (and not just built in hardware/operating system AI in phones, tablets etc), could be the next catalyst higher. But we know that AT&T is incorporating AI into many parts of the company beyond the simple examples above to enhance customer service.

The company is using generative AI, and sees a big AI opportunty. What will this do to boost the stock? Well, it will help modernize its fraud protection measures. It is doing so through advanced pattern recognition. By using all the data it has coming in, the generative AI translates this into information to help find possible complex fraud patterns, allowing the company to intervene quickly to prevent a larger problem. Taking it further, the AI tools that are crunching all the data (a tool known as Databricks) can also test scenarios and AT&T can train its AI models on the latest possible threats to improve accuracy. Another buzz word? Large language models. AT&T is using Meta Llama 3 and Anthropic to get patterns on customer and agent interaction to help not only look for fraud, but to look over customer sentiment and help determine better promotions, what is working and not working etc. We think ultimately this can improve margins in the long-term.

On top of that AI can be used to help make network operations better. How? Well with AI tools AT&T can forecast times when network traffic may be high or low, help predict capacity needs, and down the road, help to optimize where the company places network infrastructure. Imagine not running into dead zones or spotty/slow coverage down the road. While we could not find any hard data on this, there are tons of social media posts and forum posts online about people complaining about spotty coverage and wanting to switch service. A simple internet search reveals this. So, AI could help mitigate this. AI will also be used for threat detection and cybersecurity, increase employee efficiency, and to improve other security tasks. All of this could help reduce churn, and improve margins.

There have been a few other items of interest to be aware of as well. When it comes to attracting customers, people want speed. Well AT&T fiber, which we discuss some below, as well as AT&T's wireless networks, we assessed as the fastest in the United States. This is a further selling point that the company can use to attract and retain customers.

We also want to point out: Nokia (NOK) just entered into a new deal with AT&T. This is a multi-year agreement and it will be done to upgrade Nokia's voice carriage and 5G network automation in the U.S. And this relates a bit to AI, through the cloud tech AT&T is using. The updates being done are being done to Nokia's IMS Voice Core, allowing for increased automation. This will boost AT&T’s time it takes to get to market on new service offerings, and help with cost savings.

In another interesting technological development, AT&T just completed a video call by satellite, in partnership with AST SpaceMobile (ASTS). This is an advancement in boosting connectivity and reliability, particularly in rural areas. More on this can be read here.

Making it easier for shared accounts to pay their bill, AT&T just introduced a new way to pay, called SplitPay. Essentially customers that have multiple lines or multiple people relying on the same account can be sent a new and unique link that allows them to pay their portion of the bill. This is a big boost to convenience for account holders, and helps ensure bills are paid on time for AT&T. In theory, this too can reduce churn.

Recent performance and outlook ahead

Revenue in Q4 were $32.3 billion and was ahead of analyst consensus by $360 million. The company had a good quarter overall, though the one anchor continues to be business lines. But mobility and fiber are very strong. Mobility revenues for Q4 were $23.1 billion, up 3.3%. We also look at net adds. Postpaid net adds were a strong 839,000, with phone net adds of 482,000. We did see prepaid net losses of 136,000 however, and prepaid phone net losses of 119,000. We also saw equipment sales were also up 3.3% this year from last year, and that is largely due to new phone offerings. And as we mentioned, AI could reduce churn, but it is already quite low. Postpaid churn came at just 0.85%. All positive. However we saw business wireline revenue down 10%. There is weaker demand for legacy voice and data services, and it is not unique to AT&T as competitors are seeing declines broadly speaking. We Fiber is so strong, with 307,000 net adds. Broadband net adds were also 123,000. All in all, a solid performance on these key metrics.

We won't go into much detail here as you can review Q4 earnings coverage from our firm or colleagues on Seeking Alpha, but we will mention that EPS came in at $0.56 per share, stronger than predicted. The key metric we always watch is free cash flow. AT&T generated free cash flow of $4.8 billion in Q4. This more than covered the dividend and left a lot to tackle debt. There is room for a dividend hike too. However, we do think prioritizing leverage is key. Total debt was $123.5 billion to start 2025, and net debt was $120.1 billion. $5.5 billion in debt was reduced in 2024, leaving a 2.5x leverage, which is the lowest it has been in years. However, it is projected to stay around this leverage multiple for the next year or so. We think that leaves room for a hike. Even 4 cents a year would not heavily impact the coverage ratio or the ability to invest and/or pay debt. The guide showed free cash flow wont' expand in 2025, but is still sizable.

Some risks to be aware of

We mentioned the debt above, but that is a risk. A business downturn can mean less cash flow, and the debt must be paid. Interest must be paid. That could lead to risks to the dividend. With a higher for longer rate environment rate refinancing near-term will come with higher payments. We also know that there can really be no significant cuts to capital expenditures long-term, because to keep a competitive advantage, the company has to constantly innovate and update its offerings and infrastructure. There are needs for spectrum auctions which come at heavy costs. The business wireline segment continues to erode. We do not see this reversing anytime soon, so that remains a headwind to be aware of. Regulatory changes can always impact costs. While the current U.S. administration is more business friendly, regulations can impact the balance sheet. So do lawsuits, as well as economic impacts. Recall, there are still issues with lead lines in the ground from many years ago, though AT&T has some facts on the health and safety impacts. But in the immediate term, competition remains the biggest risk. The more the other big telecoms take customers or reduce their own churn, the less revenue AT&T can potentially see.

Looking ahead

As we look ahead, AI incorporation can improve margins and increase retention, while preventing fraud and promoting security. Optimizing cell service coverage is a key to reducing churn. AT&T is sees revenue growth in the low single digits in 2025, with fiber revenue growth in the mid-teens, and mobility revenue growth in the low single-digits. it was positive to see a guide for adjusted EBITDA to expand 3%-plus. The EPS figure is seen at $1.97 to $2.07 (down from 2024 due to offloading DirecTV). That key free cash flow metric will be "at least $16 billion", providing significant dividend coverage, and we would argue, room for an increase. The stock in our opinion while having made a run, is still a good hold. But, shaving SOME profit does make sense after the run, it is just prudent position management. This comes as there are many lucrative shorter-term opportunities to put money to work, and capture moves like we got with AT&T over the last year.

data.title: DUHP: A Promising Quality ETF, But Not The Best
data.description:
DUHP strategy

Dimensional US High Profitability ETF (NYSEARCA:DUHP) is an actively managed fund launched on 02/23/2022, with a portfolio of 167 stocks, a dividend yield of 1.09% and a total expense ratio of 0.22%. As described in the prospectus by Dimensional, the fund is focused on…

…large U.S. companies that the Advisor determines to have high profitability relative to other U.S. large cap companies at the time of purchase. An equity issuer is considered to have high profitability because it has high earnings or profits from operations in relation to its book value or assets.

Additionally, positions may be increased, reduced or closed based on momentum and short-run reversals. Nonetheless, the portfolio turnover rate is very low: 4% in the most recent fiscal year. This article will use as a benchmark the Russell 1000 index, represented by iShares Russell 1000 ETF (IWB).

Portfolio

The portfolio is mostly invested in large and mega caps companies (about 74% of asset value), with a focus on technology (27.1%) and significant exposure in industrials (17.2%) and healthcare (15.5%). Other sectors are below 12%. Compared to the Russell 1000, DUHP mostly overweights industrials, healthcare, and consumer staples. It ignores real estate and significantly downplays communication services.

The portfolio is quite concentrated, but not excessively. The top 10 holdings, listed in the next table, represent 38.5% of asset value and the five heaviest positions weigh between 4% and 6%.

Fundamentals

Valuation ratios are mixed. DUHP is slightly pricier than the Russell 1000 based on the price/earnings ratio, and cheaper based on price/sales and price/cash flow. The price/book ratio is much higher than the benchmark’s, but it is one of the least reliable fundamental metrics and I would ignore it. Aggregate growth rates are also mixed and not far from the benchmark.

data.title: Swiss Re: Higher Dividend And Solid Execution, Buy Confirmed
data.description:
Taking advantage of our EU insurance coverage, in early January 2025, we initiated coverage of Swiss Re AG (OTCPK:SSREF, OTCPK:SSREY). The reinsurance sector has observed a material positive crowding movement, with Swiss Re being our preferred reinsurer player based on 1) a supportive MACRO environment creating a positive virtuous cycle as human beings become more risk-averse, insurers experience solid sales, prompting them to turn to reinsurers to offload portions of their risk, 2) conservatism in 2025 guidance, and 3) a solid total return ahead. Since our buy rating, Swiss Re's shares are up by almost 10% (Fig 1).

Fig 1

FY Results and Our Positive Take

The company delivered a 2024 net profit of $3.2 billion with a RoE of 15% and a net income of $1.1 billion in Q4 (Fig 2). A lower combined ratio and group items mainly drove the Q4 results. In detail, on a yearly basis, P&C Reinsurance's net income reached $1.2 billion with a combined ratio of 89.9%. Cost of Risks reached 80.9% and was 300 basis points better than consensus estimates. This was supported by lower nat cat loss in the quarter. These positive results were also evident in the Corporate Solutions profit, which reached $829 million with a combined ratio of 89.7%. The Life & Health Reinsurance segment also reported a net income of $1.5 billion. Swiss Re is well diversified across segments and on a GEO basis. This readout, particularly on a 2025 loss update, adds credibility to Swiss Re 2025 results and boosts our confidence in the company's equity investment story. Given our insurance sector knowledge, we also report two additional ratios:

  • Swiss Re ROI reached 4.0% with a recurring income yield of 4.0% (and a reinvestment yield of 4.6% in Q4);
  • The group solvency ratio remains above the target range of 200- 250% and reached 257% (Fig 3).

Fig 2

Fig 3

Why are we positive?

  1. Swiss Re's initial guidance around the California Wildfire loss has assuaged investor concerns. We view the FY results as a solid update, with a better-than-expected California wildfire loss. The initial estimate was set at more than $1 billion, and now the company forecasts a loss of <$700 million based on a $40 billion industry. According to our calculation, this represents <35% of the Fiscal Year natural catastrophe budget (better outcome than some peers such as Munich Re set at 40%);
  2. There was also a marginal dividend per share beat. The Swiss RE Board of Directors has proposed an 8% DPS increase to $7.35 (Fig 4). In our initial estimates, we were reporting a dividend uplift of plus 7% year over year.
  3. The company's Solvency Ratio reached 257% on a reported basis. We should report that Swiss RE now has much lower interest rate sensitivities. This offers downside protection;
  4. On the standalone business, the company noted that P&C Re increased premium volume by 7.0% in the January 2025 renewals, combined with the underlying Fiscal Year cost of risk of 83% (vs. guided <85% in 2025), which increases our confidence on net income delivery. There was no change to the company's outlook (Fig 5); however, Swiss RE targets a >$4.4 billion net profit. This would represent a net income increase of at least 37.5% on a yearly basis.

Fig 4

Fig 5

Financials and Valuation

Even without incorporating our more optimistic 2025 guidance — compared to the company's latest projections with net profit estimates above $4.4 billion (Fig 5) — the stock is trading at a price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio of around 10x. In our estimates, we believe the company could reach a profit of $4.6 billion with an EPS of CHF 13.47. Our premium growth assumption was just +3%; however, the company increased its premium by 7%. Considering the combined ratio evolution, net profit estimates are not priced in. Additionally, Swiss Re valuation remains notably lower than its European counterparts, which generally trade at a 12x P/E multiple (excluding Zurich Insurance, which commands a premium - Fig 6). By applying this sector-average multiple, we increased our Swiss Re fair value from CHF 152.4 to 161.5 per share ($45 per ADR), compared to the current market price of CHF 147. Furthermore, factoring in total return, including dividends and share buybacks, Swiss Re offers an attractive 5% dividend yield, and again, we might expect a buyback of at least CHF 1 billion, considering a 70% payout ratio. Given its CHF-denominated nature, Swiss Re is likely to remain an appealing choice for institutional investors.

Fig 6

Risks

Given our recent initiation of coverage, we report the following risks: 1) a volatile financial market with significant government, corporate bonds, and equities investments. That said, the company lowered its interest sensitivity risks; 2) Swiss RE is exposed to credit changes and debtor default risk; 3) Natural Catastrophes are unpredictable, and the company is subject to claims (the California Wildfire loss proved to be a harmful event with a 35% of Swiss Re natural catastrophe yearly budget); 4) Nat Cat risks also result in litigation costs; 5) lower reinvestment yield (again, this was not the case in Q4 2024 results); and 6) regulatory changes such as higher corporate tax and capital requirements.

Conclusion

We are not surprised to see a modestly positive stock reaction. Wall Street analysts are still carrying a conservatism on the company's 2025 guidance, but we already see signs of improvements in premium pricing. Additionally, the company grew its dividend per share by 8% (vs. a previous estimate of +7%). Swiss Re remains a buy with solid fundamentals and a discount vs. peers.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Nippon Express: Why I Am Upgrading From Sell To Strong Buy
data.description:
Nippon Express Holdings, Inc. (OTCPK:NPEHF, OTCPK:NEXHY) is one of the companies for which I added coverage in November. Despite increasing focus on the growth potential in India, the business remained unappealing to me. The stock lacked appeal to me due to the low-margin nature of the business.

In this report, I discuss whether the stock has become more attractive. I will also update my price target for the name.

Since Nippon Express is not known by everyone, I have included an introduction to the company below. If you are familiar with the company, you may skip to the discussion of the most recent earnings.

Nippon Express Is The 8th Largest Global Freight Forwarder In The World

Nippon Express was founded in 1937 and employs more than 78,000 people. The company is active in 50 countries and ranks as the 8th largest global freight forwarder in the world.

The company focuses on all modes of transportation, meaning air, water, ground, and railway freight. Apart from that, the company also is active in specialized transport, which includes heavy transport, fine arts transport and moving services.

Nippon Express owns warehouses in key markets such as China, India, Indonesia, Australia, Brazil, Mexico, the US, and various countries in Europe. Through these warehouses it provides global logistics services, 3PL (third-party logistics) services and value-added services such as inspections, packing, and control management.

Nippon Express Misses On Guidance For 2024

The table above is not the prettiest one and might require a brief introduction. The Japan segment represents the domestic market. The four geographical areas below that are aggregated into the overseas segment. While security transportation, heavy haulage and logistics complete the list of reporting segments within the business.

The results were not great. Nippon Express had guided for ¥25.7 billion in sales. It ended up reporting ¥25.776 billion in sales. While it did report higher than anticipated revenues, it missed its ¥700 million profit target by 9.2%. Consequently, the operating margin was 2.5%, which is 0.2 points lower than the target.

In Japan, the company exceeded the sales target by ¥100 million. However, this was fully offset by a ¥116 million miss in the overseas segment. The main reason why the company exceeded the sales target was due to results in logistics support and heavy haulage.

The challenging cost environment with little space to pass through higher costs to customers is also reflected in the results. Despite the domestic business posting stronger than expected sales, it fell short by ¥29 million in profits. The overseas segment missed targets by ¥33 million.

So, the market environment is challenging and dynamic. Given that the company missed the targets it set in November is definitely not a convincing performance.

Nippon Express Guides For Mid-Single Digit Growth And Margin Expansion

For 2025, Nippon Express is aiming to increase its revenues by 4.7% to ¥27 billion while increasing its profits to ¥800 million. This indicates a margin of 3%. It once again shows the thin-margin nature of the business. At the same time, due to the late miss in FY24, I do not entirely ignore the guidance. However, note that it missed the guidance last year, even though it was confirmed six weeks before the reporting period would end.

Nippon Express Is Unattractive For Investment

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener which uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions, and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

The price target has been calculated based on the listing of Nippon Express in Japan, which provides the most liquid way to invest in the stock.

I am marking Nippon Express as a strong buy with a $23.79 price target. Which is a massive upgrade from the previous sell rating. Part of the reason is that we are now valuing the stock according to the listing in Japan.

The other reason is that the CAGRs on EBITDA and free cash flow have significantly increased. Previously the CAGRs were 5.4% and 2.6%. The most recent analyst estimates suggest 7.3% and 5.8%. This unlocks a significant upside. I believe a positive growth story for e-commerce forwarding through air supports a significant upside for the overseas segment results.

We also see that the company is fairly valued against 2024 earnings, which means there is a potentially attractive entry point with eyes on growth in future years.

Conclusion: Nippon Express Is A Strong Buy Despite Margin Risk

I continue to highlight the thin-margin nature of the forwarding business. In 2024, we saw how the company missed the targets that it had laid out just a month earlier. So, we definitely do believe there is a continued risk of margin pressure due to the competitive nature of the business. Other risks to consider are the currency risk as the company has international exposure but reports its results in JPY.

However, with the most recent growth estimates and results in, I am upgrading Nippon Express Holdings, Inc. stock from sell to strong buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Innovative Industrial Properties: An Opportunity To Start Or Add To A Position
data.description:
Innovative Industrial Properties, Inc (NYSE:IIPR) is the first real estate investment trust (REIT) on the New York Stock Exchange that acquires and manages specialized properties leased to licensed operators for regulated cannabis cultivation. IIPR is a pioneer in the industry, breaking onto the scene in 2016. IIPR operates as a triple net lease REIT by providing cannabis operators capital, property, and infrastructure in exchange for rent/lease payments. Within eight years of operation, IIPR has accumulated 108 properties comprising approximately 9.0 million rentable square feet in 19 states.

IIPR has a promising future, they have a strong balance sheet, pay a juicy dividend, and have a management team of highly experienced real estate professionals. My opinion is these are all reasons to buy the stock today. However, the stock has experienced a significant drop in price in the last couple of months. This drop in price is a result of one of their largest tenants defaulting on their rent payments. I will acknowledge this default puts their short-term future at risk. However, I think this is a great opportunity to begin a new position or add to an existing one.

IIPR Balance Sheet

When considering a REIT company and if it is a good investment to purchase their stock, the balance sheet, in my opinion, is the most important financial statement available. Real estate is based on leverage, properties are not typically purchased with 100% of cash and a company or individual will use a loan to finance the purchase. When times get hard and the economy turns, having a significant amount of leverage on your balance sheet can hurt you. Understanding the amount of leverage a REIT has is valuable in understanding the health of the company. Let's take a brief look at the leverage of IIPR.

When analyzing a balance sheet, there are multiple leverage ratios to consider. One leverage ratio we will take a look at is the debt to asset ratio. The debt to asset ratio compares the total debt the company carries relative to the total assets on the balance sheet. Understanding how much debt the company has relative to their assets will help us understand how constrained IIPR's liquidity may be if times get tough and property values decrease or if the company needs to seek additional funding. According to the most recent annual report released on February 21st, 2025 IIPR has approximately $297 million in long-term debt. Compared to total assets of approximately $2.37 billion. The debt to asset ratio of IIPR is approximately 13%. Indicating, 13% of their asset value is leveraged. Indicating a very healthy debt to asset ratio.

Another leverage ratio we will look at is the debt to equity ratio. The debt to equity ratio helps can help indicate potential risks to the financial health and indicates whether a company relying on more debt or equity to finance operations. The debt to equity of IIPR is approximately 15%. Indicating IIPR is potentially relying more on equity to finance operations and investment rather than debt. However, viewing the debt to equity ratio alone does not tell the whole story. Comparing the ratio to industry peers can provide insight into how IIPR is managing their balance sheet relative to the industry. Comparing IIPR debt to equity ratios to other high profile REITs; Prologis (PLD) is 56%, Realty Income (O) is 68%, and Simon Property Group (SPG) is 824%. Comparatively speaking, IIPR has a very low debt to equity ratio. Indicating IIPR is relying less on debt to finance operations relative to their peers. Also indicating IIPR is putting themselves at less financial risk, relative to peers, when accessing the debt markets. Again indicating a healthy balance sheet and management of resources.

Dividend Payout

IIPR pays a considerable dividend with a current annual, forward, dividend rate of $7.60, purchasing shares today will yield you an approximate 10.3% yield. It is commonly preached that a high yield indicates a potentially troubled company (i.e. yield trap). Now, granted, the yield has increased substantially recently due to the sharp decline in stock price (which we will address here soon). However, the dividend is strong and is why one should consider purchasing this stock.

When considering REITs, I prefer to consider dividends relative to Funds from Operations (FFO) or Adjusted FFO (AFFO). For REITs, AFFO is the measure of the operating performance and the companies' ability to generate cash similar to free cash flow of a company. The AFFO is how much money the REIT can pay out, understanding that REITs have a requirement to pay out a percentage of its taxable income as dividends. According to the press release for the fourth quarter of 2024, IIPR paid a quarterly dividend of $1.90 and reported normalized AFFO of $2.22 - indicating IIPR had an 85% dividend payout ratio relative to the AFFO. IIPR's payout ratio is a bit higher than what I would prefer (less than 80% of FFO or AFFO) however at 85% there is still enough margin to maintain the dividend. For comparison, Prologis has a forward dividend rate of $4.04 and recent FFO of $5.56. Indicating a payout ratio of approximately 73% of FFO.

In my opinion, an 85% dividend payout ratio indicated a somewhat safe dividend. The 10% for IIPR does not indicate a dividend trap, rather, it is my opinion it indicates unfavorable sentiment and uncertainty related to the cannabis industry. In addition, IIPR has increased its dividend each year since inception (2016) almost a 10-year streak, however with an 85% payout ratio, significant near term dividend increases should not be expected.

Management Team

When it comes to Real Estate Investing, I frequent the management team page to understand who is running the company. For IIPR, the C-Suite and even the board comprises very experienced real estate professionals. Here I want to highlight a few members of the management team and why having them lead the company should let you sleep well at night.

The Chief Executive Officer (CEO), Paul Smither, has served as the CEO since the formation of IIPR. Paul has previous experience working for an aerospace manufacturer and, prior to working in manufacturing, worked as a lawyer in the commercial and real estate transaction space. The Chief Operating Officer (COO) of the organization, Catherine Hastings, has been at the company since 2017. She has held many roles internally, including Chief Financial Officer, treasurer, and Chief Accounting Officer. Prior to her time here at IIPR, Catherine was a Vice President at BioMed Realty Trust, Inc. a REIT specializing in acquiring, leasing, developing, and managing laboratory and office space for the life science industry. Another heavily regulated, niche, real estate industry.

Last but not least, the anchor of the management team; Alan Gold. Alan currently serves as the Executive Chairman and frankly, IIPR could not have a better Executive Chairman to lead this board. Alan has decades of real estate experience. Previously, Alan was Executive Chairman of IQHQ, Inc (IQHQ); Chairman, President; CEO of BioMed Realty Trust, Inc. (BioMed); and Co-Founder and President of Alexandria Real Estate Equities, Inc (ARE). The companies, IQHQ, BioMed, and ARE all operate in the life sciences industry, an industry known to have high regulations and very specialized properties, indicating Alan has had extensive experience being a manager in a high regulated and specialized real estate industry.

The management team is highly experienced and very capable of navigating the murky waters of the cannabis industry. In my opinion, all faith can be placed into the team to lead IIPR through tough times and good times.

Recent News Leads to Significant Price Drop

On December 20, 2024, IIPR reported defaults by PharmaCann Inc. (PharmaCann) on six of 11 leases. The default occurred during the month of December and totals $4.2 million in rent, property management fees, taxes, and insurance payments. Following this news, IIPR shares gapped down about 22% as the market digested the news. The default by PharmaCann is significant, understanding the company represents approximately 17% of IIPR's total rental revenues.

About a month after the news broke, IIPR reported a resolution to defaults by PharmaCann. The resolution generally included the following:

  • IIPR applies security deposits held under all leases for the payment of defaulted rents in December 2024 and January 2025;
  • Existing leases at nine of the 11 properties were amended, increasing total required security deposits and reducing base rent by 7%;
  • Select properties will be transitioned to new tenants by August 1, 2025;
  • Additional equity capital contributed to PharmaCann and issuance of a secured promissory note from PharmaCann to IIPR; and
  • PharmaCann is not allowed to incur additional indebtedness without IIPR's consent.

The rapid response to the defaults and the in-depth resolution established by IIPR represents a masterclass in tenant management. I believe this exemplifies the leadership abilities of the management. However, given the additional equity capital and promissory note, IIPR is further rooting their relationship with PharmaCann their largest tenant. This may prove to be costly if PharmaCann is not able to improve their business.

An Opportunity to Purchase a High Yielder

Even though the default by PharmaCann indicates the fragile nature of the cannabis industry, it does not take away from the overall health and strength of IIPR as a REIT. IIPR has multiple tenants, and PharmaCann only represents 16.3% of the annualized base rent. They have a diverse base of tenants in multiple different states. Additionally, IIPR is not in the cannabis industry directly, they simply provide land and infrastructure to operators. The cannabis industry is mature enough, there will always be operators looking to lease space and buildings.

IIPR's leadership has exemplified their ability to maintain a strong balance sheet and navigate difficult situations while providing exceptional shareholder returns. With IIPR's balance sheet, I do not expect a dividend cut to occur due to PharmaCann's defaults. In their most recent quarterly report on February 19th, there was no mention of a dividend cut.

Buying IIPR right now gives you a solid 10% yield that, I believe, will continue to be increased in the future. Additionally, the stock is at 52-week lows and, barring any other news of tenant rent defaults, I would not expect to go any lower. However, the investor should expect to experience more volatility in the stock price until the cannabis industry receives more clear guidance from the federal government.

data.title: Compass Pathways: Betting On The Future Of Psilocybin Therapy
data.description:
Skepticism, regulatory hurdles, and high cash burn have characterized the psychedelic biotech sector in the recent past. Most investors have thrown COMPASS Pathways plc (NASDAQ:CMPS) into the same ailing basket, betting that its psilocybin-based treatment will meet the fate of other speculative biotech plays. But behind the noise, I believe Compass is positioning itself to take advantage of regulatory tailwinds, compelling clinical data, and a financial cushion that goes well beyond its upcoming catalysts. But even as broader sentiment remains bearish in the space, this mispricing presents a window into opportunities for investors willing to look beyond short-term volatility.

Latest Financials

Compass Pathways reported a net loss of $155.1 million for 2024 compared to a $118.5 million loss in 2023. That's in line with its late-stage clinical progress, as R&D spending jumped to $119 million from $87.5 million last year. The company clearly is funneling capital into its Phase 3 trials for COMP360, which often squeeze cash reserves but are necessary for any biotech moving toward commercialization. General and administrative expenses also grew to $59.2 million due to increased headcount and legal costs as the company positioned itself better for regulatory milestones and possible partnerships. Of course, rising costs are not unheard of in pre-revenue biotech firms, but the question is whether Compass has the financial runway to keep its ambitions without a dilutive raise in the near term.

End of 2024, cash reserves were $165.1 million, down from $220.2 million a year earlier. Given an estimated cash burn of $120 million to $145 million by 2025, Compass has about 4 to 6 quarters of runway before needing another capital infusion. But the company raised another $140.4 million in early 2025, giving it time to deliver data readouts that could change its valuation narrative. If its next Phase 3 results show strong efficacy, Compass might be able to raise non-dilutive funding in partnership rather than issuing new equity at low prices. Such a financial cushion suggests the company is not in immediate distress, but I would watch for changes in spending that accelerate cash depletion.

Psychedelic Neuroscience

Compass is targeting the two most challenging psychiatric conditions: Treatment-resistant depression and post-traumatic stress disorder, both of which have seen little progress over time in terms of viable approaches. Most traditional antidepressants such as SSRIs and SNRIs take weeks or longer before providing meaningful relief. The mechanism behind COMP360, Compass's proprietary synthetic psilocybin formulation, is quite different. Unlike conventional treatments that increase serotonin availability over time, psilocybin produces a rapid, transient psychedelic state that "resets" neural networks associated with rigid, maladaptive thought patterns. This is mediated mainly by 5-HT2A receptor agonism and results in increased neuroplasticity and temporary dismantling of the default mode network, a brain system involved in self-referential thinking and rumination - core issues in depression and PTSD.

The uniqueness of COMP360 is that it can provide therapeutic benefits with a single administration, unlike the daily dosing of most antidepressants. A one-time 25 mg dose in the PTSD trial resulted in significant reductions in Clinician-Administered PTSD Scale scores, with 77.3% maintaining response at 12 weeks. Such data suggests a durable effect that could make psilocybin-based therapies economically more attractive than chronic regimens. As opposed to other psychedelic-focused biotech firms pursuing ketamine- or MDMA-assisted therapy, Compass is betting that psilocybin can provide long-term symptom relief without the addiction risks of other compounds. Compass has the largest study of psilocybin ever conducted and is positioned to be the most clinically rigorous player in the space, potentially conferring a regulatory and commercial advantage.

COMP360’s Clinical Trajectory

Compass Pathways enters a pivotal period with the introduction of its COMP360 psilocybin therapy into late-stage trials. The company's Phase 3 program in treatment-resistant depression is the largest randomized, controlled psilocybin study to date, with key data readouts likely to shape the company and the wider psychedelic sector going forward. The first major catalyst is the six-week topline data from the COMP005 trial arriving in the second quarter of 2025, which is the primary endpoint for regulatory consideration. If results support the endurance of response observed in early trials, Compass will have an important edge in its push for FDA approval. A second large readout from COMP006 in 2026 will provide 26-week data - an important marker of long-term efficacy. Although the rapid-acting antidepressant effects of psilocybin are well documented, regulators will want to see sustained improvement before full approval.

Outside of TRD, Compass is also quietly preparing for COMP360's entry into the PTSD market, which has an even greater unmet need and no new FDA-approved treatments in more than two decades. The company's Phase 2 trial met safety and efficacy endpoints, with 63.6% of participants in remission by week four. Such findings from a small open-label study suggest that COMP360 could be generalizable to other psychiatric conditions driven by rigid, trauma-related neural patterns. With additional capital secured in early 2025, Compass can accelerate a late-stage PTSD program without losing focus on trauma recovery. The next year will determine if COMP360 becomes a legitimate breakthrough in psychiatric treatment, with regulatory clarity likely determining its trajectory in the short and long term.

Regulatory Hurdles and Scientific Doubts

COMP360 presents significant risks that investors should not ignore. The regulatory landscape for psychedelic-based treatments remains murky even with the FDA Breakthrough Therapy designation. Even though Compass has taken among the most structured clinical approaches in the sector, the agency may require longer-term safety and efficacy data before full approval. Psychedelics remain politically and culturally contentious and resistance from more conservative regulatory bodies may delay commercialization. Even if COMP360 is approved, there is no guarantee that insurers will offer reimbursement, given the therapy's unique administration model that requires intense psychological support. As opposed to standard antidepressants that can be prescribed and refilled easily with minimal oversight, psilocybin-assisted therapy involves prolonged in-clinic sessions that may limit scalability and complicate widespread adoption.

Scientists remain skeptical about psilocybin's long-term efficacy. While the Phase 2 Compass results in TRD and PTSD are promising, some academics argue that the observed improvements are at least partially related to increased suggestibility due to the increased neurobiological effect of psychedelic experiences. It is notoriously difficult to control for the placebo effect in psychedelic studies, since patients know immediately whether they have received an active dose. Other questions are related to treatment response durability. Although chronic conditions such as depression and PTSD warrant sustained symptom reduction over 12 weeks according to early data with COMP360, the need for repeat dosing is still debatable. If COMP360 ultimately requires several treatment sessions per year, its economic viability and practical feasibility might be challenged, particularly in healthcare systems unwilling to accept new psychiatric interventions. However, advocates point out that the rapid action of psilocybin therapy and its capacity to induce neuroplasticity suggest a fundamentally new paradigm in psychiatric care that may be superior to the trial-and-error of conventional antidepressants. Whether these scientific and regulatory hurdles can be overcome by Compass will decide whether it becomes a niche player or a dominant force in mental health treatment.

Current Valuation

Traditional valuation metrics are difficult to apply to pre-revenue biotech firms like Compass because earnings have yet to materialize. However, the company has a 1.39 price-to-book ratio - significantly below the sector median of 2.12 - which suggests the market is undervaluing its asset base relative to its peers.

In terms of Compass's potential, we can look at the market for its lead indication, treatment-resistant depression. MDD impacts about 16.1 million adults annually in the United States. About 30% of these - or roughly 4.8 million people - are TRD-positive. TRD has a huge economic burden - with $43.8 billion annually in healthcare costs, lost productivity, and unemployment. With regulatory approval, COMP360 could drive significant revenue from only a small slice of the total market.

Takeaways

In its current state, Compass is navigating a complex landscape where scientific promise, regulatory uncertainty, and financial pressures intersect. The company’s commitment to rigorous clinical trials sets it apart from many players in the psychedelic space, positioning COMP360 as the most credible psilocybin-based therapy in development. Its financial runway, bolstered by a recent capital raise, provides enough flexibility to reach critical data readouts, but long-term sustainability will depend on regulatory approvals and commercial adoption. While skepticism remains about the scalability and durability of psilocybin-assisted therapy, early clinical results suggest a meaningful and potentially lasting impact on treatment-resistant depression and PTSD.

If Compass successfully transitions from an experimental biotech to a commercial-stage company, I believe the current market valuation could prove to be deeply discounted relative to its long-term potential. Investors willing to tolerate near-term volatility and regulatory unpredictability may find an asymmetric risk-reward opportunity, as the company moves toward pivotal milestones in the near to mid future.
